did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for the classification of benign and malignant skin lesions (dermatology task). It also employs a hybrid approach involving Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and IGWO optimization for feature extraction and classification, along with Kohonen networks for segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of a CNN-based system for classifying benign and malignant skin lesions. It includes sections on methodology (preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification using ANN and IGWO), experimental results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score), and a conclusion, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review. Although published in conference proceedings, it reports new technical results.

title: Classification of Benign and Malignant Skin Lesions using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes a CNN-based system for the automated classification of benign and malignant skin lesions, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis. The methodology utilizes the ISIC Archive dataset, employing preprocessing steps such as resizing to 224x224 pixels and normalization. The pipeline integrates image segmentation via Kohonen networks, feature extraction using CNNs, and classification through a hybrid approach of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) optimized by Improved Grey Wolf Optimization (IGWO). The model was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score metrics on free downloadable skin image datasets. The results indicate that the proposed CNN architecture outperforms existing diagnostic techniques in speed, accuracy, and scalability for early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin lesion; explicitly listed as a category in the ISIC Archive dataset used for training and as a target for classification into malignant categories.
Malignant skin lesion; explicitly listed as a category in the ISIC Archive dataset used for training and as a target for classification into malignant categories.
Malignant skin lesion; explicitly listed as a category in the ISIC Archive dataset used for training and as a target for classification into malignant categories.
Benign skin lesion; explicitly listed as a category in the ISIC Archive dataset used for training and as a target for classification into benign categories.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (benign vs. malignant, specifically melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify skin lesions into malignant... and benign... categories'. The model uses a CNN for feature extraction and a hybrid ANN/IGWO approach for classification. The output layer uses a softmax activation function to classify input images into specific classes (benign or malignant). The system returns a classification result (malignant or benign) along with a confidence score. While segmentation is performed as a preprocessing step using Kohonen networks, the primary predictive task is classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A large repository of dermoscopic images labeled with various types of skin lesions, used as the source data for the study. The dataset contains both benign and malignant cases.

use.training: Used as training data for the CNN model.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, AUC-ROC Curve
ISIC Archive.notes: The paper states that experimental results showed the proposed model outperformed existing diagnostic techniques in speed, accuracy, and scalability. It mentions that the model was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. However, specific numerical values for these metrics are not reported in the provided text. Two specific test cases are shown with confidence scores: one with 5.6% confidence of skin cancer and another with 0.4% confidence of skin cancer.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for feature extraction; Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for classification optimized by Improved Grey Wolf Optimization (IGWO).

architecture_details.backbone: CNN with convolutional layers, ReLU activation functions, max pooling layers, and dropout layers.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layers followed by a softmax activation function for binary classification (benign vs. malignant).
architecture_details.segmentation: Kohonen network used for image segmentation to isolate the region of interest.
architecture_details.optimization: Improved Grey Wolf Optimization (IGWO) used to optimize ANN weights and biases.
architecture_details.layers: Not explicitly specified (described generally as 'several' convolutional layers and 'fully connected layers').
architecture_details.heads: Single output head for binary classification.

training_details.hyperparameters: Learning rate (scheduled at run-time), number of layers, epoch numbers, batch size (tuned via grid search or random search).
training_details.training_schedule: Multiple epochs until loss converged; Early Stopping used based on validation loss.
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, flipping, zooming, and color jittering.

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (benign or malignant) with confidence score.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a fixed resolution of 224x224 pixels
Normalization of pixel values between 0 and 1
Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, zooming, and color jittering
Image segmentation using Kohonen networks to isolate the region of interest
Noise reduction and filtering to enhance image quality and uniformity

conclusions: The proposed CNN-based system effectively classifies benign and malignant skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis) using dermoscopic images.

The model utilizes a pipeline involving Kohonen networks for segmentation, CNNs for feature extraction, and a hybrid Artificial Neural Network (ANN) optimized by Improved Grey Wolf Optimization (IGWO) for classification.
Experimental results indicate that the proposed architecture outperforms existing diagnostic techniques in terms of speed, accuracy, and scalability for early skin cancer detection.
The system provides classification results (benign or malignant) along with confidence scores, aiding dermatologists in making informed decisions.
The study demonstrates the potential of deep learning to automate and improve skin cancer diagnosis, particularly in regions with limited access to expert dermatologists.
The application is designed with a scalable architecture using React.js for the frontend and Node.js/Express.js for the backend, supporting real-time analysis and user interaction.
important_remarks: Specific quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC values) are not reported in the text; results are described only qualitatively as 'outperforming' existing techniques.

The study relies on 'free downloadable datasets' (ISIC Archive) without specifying the exact subset, split ratios, or sample counts, raising concerns about reproducibility and potential data leakage if standard splits were not used.
No external validation on independent datasets is reported; the model is only tested on the source dataset, limiting evidence of generalizability across diverse populations.
Formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is absent; the paper does not report statistical significance of the claimed improvements.
The paper acknowledges 'class imbalance' as a limitation that needs to be addressed for clinical application, suggesting potential bias in the current model's performance.

The segmentation step using Kohonen networks is described as producing 'vague and inadequate' boundaries, requiring a subsequent greedy search algorithm for refinement, which may introduce variability.
The system architecture includes a web-based deployment (React.js/Node.js) with claims of scalability, but no evidence of clinical workflow integration or user study validation is provided.
The paper notes the need for 'explainability' to enhance trust, indicating the current model lacks interpretability features critical for clinical adoption.
